title: The Polite Traveler
learningGoal: The learner will parse narrative texts identifying pragmatic markers of politeness ('Excuse me', 'Could you'), distinguishing socio-culturally appropriate L2 interactions from direct imperative phrasing.
instructions: Read this story about a man visiting London. Notice how he talks to strangers. He never gives them orders. He always starts by saying 'Excuse me' to get their attention, and 'Could you' to ask a question. Answer the 8 questions below.

contentItems.text: If he said 'Where is the train?' instead of 'Excuse me, could you tell me where the train is?', the people in London would think he is very angry!

readingText: Amit is visiting London for the first time. It is a very big, busy city. Amit is completely lost. He sees a tall man reading a newspaper. Amit does not want to be rude. He walks up to the man. 'Excuse me,' Amit says. The man looks up. 'Yes?' 'Could you tell me where the train station is?' Amit asks politely. 'Yes, of course. It is down this road,' the man says. 'Thank you very much,' Amit replies. Amit walks down the road. He feels thirsty. He goes into a small coffee shop. The woman behind the counter smiles. 'Hello, how are you today?' she asks. 'I am fine, thank you. Could you give me a bottle of water, please?' Amit asks. 'Would you like cold water or warm water?' she asks. 'Cold water, please,' Amit says. He pays for the water. He walks out of the shop and stops a taxi. 'Excuse me, could you take me to the hotel?' he asks the driver. Amit is a very polite traveler, so everyone is happy to help him.
textInTargetLanguage: Amit is visiting London for the first time. It is a very big, busy city. Amit is completely lost. He sees a tall man reading a newspaper. Amit does not want to be rude. He walks up to the man. 'Excuse me,' Amit says. The man looks up. 'Yes?' 'Could you tell me where the train station is?' Amit asks politely. 'Yes, of course. It is down this road,' the man says. 'Thank you very much,' Amit replies. Amit walks down the road. He feels thirsty. He goes into a small coffee shop. The woman behind the counter smiles. 'Hello, how are you today?' she asks. 'I am fine, thank you. Could you give me a bottle of water, please?' Amit asks. 'Would you like cold water or warm water?' she asks. 'Cold water, please,' Amit says. He pays for the water. He walks out of the shop and stops a taxi. 'Excuse me, could you take me to the hotel?' he asks the driver. Amit is a very polite traveler, so everyone is happy to help him.
baseLanguageSupportText: अमित पहली बार लंदन का दौरा कर रहा है। यह एक बहुत बड़ा, व्यस्त शहर है। अमित पूरी तरह से खो गया है। वह एक लंबे आदमी को अखबार पढ़ते हुए देखता है। अमित अशिष्ट नहीं होना चाहता। वह उस आदमी के पास जाता है। 'माफ़ कीजिए,' अमित कहता है। आदमी ऊपर देखता है। 'हाँ?' 'क्या आप मुझे बता सकते हैं कि रेलवे स्टेशन कहाँ है?' अमित विनम्रता से पूछता है। 'हाँ, बिल्कुल। यह इस सड़क के नीचे है,' आदमी कहता है। 'बहुत-बहुत धन्यवाद,' अमित जवाब देता है। अमित सड़क पर चलता है। उसे प्यास लगती है। वह एक छोटी सी कॉफी शॉप में जाता है। काउंटर के पीछे की महिला मुस्कुराती है। 'नमस्ते, आज आप कैसे हैं?' वह पूछती है। 'मैं ठीक हूँ, धन्यवाद। क्या आप कृपया मुझे पानी की एक बोतल दे सकती हैं?' अमित पूछता है। 'क्या आप ठंडा पानी चाहते हैं या गर्म पानी?' वह पूछती है। 'ठंडा पानी, कृपया,' अमित कहता है। वह पानी के लिए भुगतान करता है। वह दुकान से बाहर निकलता है और एक टैक्सी रोकता है। 'माफ़ कीजिए, क्या आप मुझे होटल ले जा सकते हैं?' वह ड्राइवर से पूछता है। अमित एक बहुत ही विनम्र यात्री है, इसलिए हर कोई उसकी मदद करने में खुश है।

exampleSentence.targetText: Excuse me, where is the station?
exampleSentence.transliteration: एक्सक्यूज़ मी, वेयर इज़ द स्टेशन?

exampleSentence.targetText: Could you take me to the hotel?
exampleSentence.transliteration: कुड यू टेक मी टू द होटल?
exampleSentence.baseTranslation: क्या आप मुझे होटल ले जा सकते हैं?

exampleSentence.targetText: Amit does not want to be rude.
exampleSentence.transliteration: अमित डज़ नॉट वांट टू बी रूड।

sentenceSupportPairs.targetText: Amit does not want to be rude.
sentenceSupportPairs.transliteration: अमित डज़ नॉट वांट टू बी रूड।

sentenceSupportPairs.targetText: Excuse me, could you tell me...?
sentenceSupportPairs.transliteration: एक्सक्यूज़ मी, कुड यू टेल मी...?
sentenceSupportPairs.baseTranslation: माफ़ कीजिए, क्या आप मुझे बता सकते हैं...?

sentenceSupportPairs.targetText: Could you give me a bottle of water, please?
sentenceSupportPairs.transliteration: कुड यू गिव मी अ बॉटल ऑफ वाटर, प्लीज़?
sentenceSupportPairs.baseTranslation: क्या आप कृपया मुझे पानी की एक बोतल दे सकती हैं?

comprehensionQuestions.questionText: What word does Amit use to comfortably grab the tall man's attention?
Instead of ordering 'Tell me where the station is', what magic phrase does Amit use?
How does Amit properly reply when the coffee lady asks 'How are you today?' ?
True or False: Saying 'Give me a bottle of water' is polite English.
What word does Amit add to the end of his request for water to make it extra polite?
When the lady asks which water he wants, how does Amit reply?
Why is everyone happy to help Amit?
options: Because he is a polite traveler

correctAnswers.q8: Because he is a polite traveler

expectedSummaryPoints: The learner recognizes 'Excuse me' as the primary interlocutor disruption mechanism.
The learner identifies 'Could you' as the modal requisite for framing requests without imperative bluntness.

feedbackRules.onPass: Magnificent! You perfectly identified the structures of English politeness.
feedbackRules.onFail: Keep trying! Remember, direct orders are considered 'Rude'. Amit never gives an order. He asks questions using 'Could you'.
feedbackRules.onPartial: Good effort! Always remember to add 'Please' to your requests and 'Thank you' to your answers.

audioAssets.audioTranscript: Amit is visiting London for the first time... (full transcript provided in text)

hintText: Look for the magic words: 'Excuse me', 'Could you', 'Please', and 'Thank you'. They are everywhere in this story!
commonMistakes: Assuming 'I am fine' is sufficient, forgetting the mandatory 'thank you' appendage in polite discourse.
